summary: O'Neil reviews a research paper examining how opinions and conviction levels propagate through social interactions. The study found that while people update their opinions when hearing similar views from others with strong convictions, they tend to ignore very different opinions. Crucially, conviction levels always rise through interaction but never fall, meaning that after many exchanges, high conviction correlates with consensus rather than correctness. The simulation revealed two attractor states: small groups of confident 'experts' (who may be wrong) and large majorities with weak but shared opinions.
key_points: People update opinions when hearing similar views with stronger conviction, but largely ignore very different opinions
Conviction rises when people hear agreement but doesn't fall when they hear disagreement, leading to high confidence regardless of accuracy
After many interactions, conviction correlates with consensus, not correctness
Two stable states emerge: influential 'experts' with strong convictions (regardless of accuracy) and large agreeing majorities
Random noise in the population weakens both the expert effect and majority effect
money_quotes: At the end of the day, after many interactions, the convictions of the group always ended up high even if the answer was wrong.
Conviction correlated to consensus rather than correctness after a few interactions.
In the presence of not much information, strong convictions might just imply lots of local agreement.
Small groups of 'experts,' defined as people with very strong convictions but who were not necessarily correct (think Larry Summers).
The 'I heard this so many times it must be true' effect seems to be directly in line with the agenda of Fox News.
If you're trying to effect opinions, you might either go with 'informing and educating the general public' or 'building up a sufficient squad of experts.'
key_insight: Social dynamics cause conviction to correlate with consensus rather than truth, since hearing agreement strengthens beliefs while disagreement is simply ignored.
